criteria: The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the Deficit Disorder Attention (DDA) problem, including how attention scores are biased toward the language modality and how cross-modal attention decays exponentially layer-by-layer, formulated as E_DDA = Π_l γ^l ε_l.

The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the MODA mechanism including the correct-after-align strategy, the Duplex Attention Alignment (V-Aligner and T-Aligner), and the Modular Attention Mask (MDM) components.

The agent has read Section 4.2 to understand all experiment settings: CLIP ViT-L/14 as visual encoder, Llama-3-Instruct-8B for 8B and Hermes2-Yi-34B for 34B as LLM backbones, AdamW optimizer, cosine LR schedule, LR 2e-5 for LLM and 2e-6 for visual encoder, warmup rate 0.03, batch size 2048, 1 epoch training.

The agent has read Section 4.3 and Table 1 to understand the component-wise ablation design: (a) individual vs. joint use of MDM and DAA, (b) attention alignment variants (MLP, +2xMLP, +GeLU, +CoV), (c) fusion variants (X_p, X_a, Con, Add), and (d) mask variants (Inf, Fix, Attn., [M]).

The plan includes implementing the MODA attention module that splits multimodal attention into self-modal and cross-modal computation paths, each with its own modulated attention mask, replacing standard self-attention in each transformer block.

The plan includes implementing Duplex Attention Alignment (DAA) with V-Aligner and T-Aligner, using Gram matrices of key states to map tokens across modalities and fuse the aligned tokens with original tokens via concatenation.

The plan includes implementing the Modular Attention Mask (MDM) with separate masks for self-modal and cross-modal attention, incorporating pseudo-attention scores with decay rate β and modality location priors derived from the normed Gram matrix.

The plan specifies training MODA with LoRA-based tuning and token merging for the cross-modal fuser, using AdamW with cosine LR schedule, differential learning rates (2e-5 for LLM, 2e-6 for visual encoder), batch size 2048, warmup 0.03, and 1 epoch.

The plan includes running the ablation study with 700K data samples at 8B scale to verify contributions of MDM and DAA individually and jointly, across general QA, knowledge QA, OCR&Chart QA, and vision-centric QA tasks.

The plan includes running perception benchmark experiments on the 16 benchmarks across four categories (general, knowledge, OCR&Chart, vision-centric) comparing MODA against LLaVA-NeXT, Mini-Gemini-HD, and Cambrian-1 at both 8B and 34B scales.

The plan includes running cognition experiments on MMRole benchmark covering 8 evaluation aspects, and emotion experiments on MVSA-S, MVSA-M, TumEmo, and HFM benchmarks reporting both ACC and F1 metrics.

The Gram matrix for each modality m is computed as G^m = K^{m⊤} K^m (inner product of key states), then normalized as ||G^m||, where K^m are the key states of the m-th modality tokens and N_m is the number of tokens in modality m (Equation 5).

The cross-modal token transfer is implemented as K^{m̄→m} = K^{m̄} ||G^m|| (Equation 6), which maps the key representations from other modality m̄ into the space of modality m using the normed Gram matrix as a kernelized mapping function.

The V-Aligner maps visual tokens to text modality space using the normed Gram matrix of text key states, and the T-Aligner maps text tokens to visual modality space using the normed Gram matrix of visual key states, forming the duplex (bidirectional) alignment.

The aligned cross-modal tokens K^{m̄→m} are fused with the original tokens using concatenation (Con), as selected by the ablation study in Table 1(c) as the best-performing fusion strategy over additive fusion (Add), X_p, and X_a.

The Duplex Attention Alignment uses the covariance (CoV) variant as the final alignment design, where the Gram matrix serves as covariance-like basis representation, maintaining linear complexity to token number by performing matrix sum only in the first round.

The self-modal attention is computed as O_self = Softmax(Q^m K^{m⊤}/τ + M^m) V^m with modality-specific mask M^m, and cross-modal attention as O_cross = Softmax(Q^m K^{m̄⊤}/τ + M^{m̄}) V^{m̄} with cross-modal mask M^{m̄} (Equations 7-8).

The Modular Attention Mask assigns pseudo-attention scores to excess positions in each row: p_base = 0, p_ij = p_base - (j-1)β (Equation 10), where each row i has n-i pseudo-attention scores allocated to positions beyond the attended sequence length n (Equation 9).

The modular attention mask uses the normed Gram matrix as an indicator to identify which tokens should carry modality location priors, enabling separate control of intra-modal and inter-modal token flow through the mask split into M^m (self) and M^{m̄} (cross).

The final MODA attention mask uses the adaptive attention-based (Attn.) masking variant selected from ablation Table 1(d), as opposed to infinity masking (Inf), fixed-value masking (Fix), or [M]-token masking.
CLIP (ViT-L/14) is configured as the visual encoder to extract visual token representations, consistent with the experiment settings used in Cambrian-1.
The model is implemented at two scales: 8B using Llama-3-Instruct-8B as the LLM backbone, and 34B using Hermes2-Yi-34B as the LLM backbone, with MODA integrated into each.
LoRA-based tuning is applied to develop the cross-modal token fuser, together with token merging to reduce computational cost and maintain linear complexity during the duplex alignment stage.

Training is configured with AdamW optimizer, cosine learning rate schedule, learning rate 2e-5 for the LLM parameters and 2e-6 for the visual encoder, warmup rate 0.03, batch size 2048, and 1 training epoch.

The MODA attention block is integrated into each transformer layer of the MLLM (as shown in Figure 3(b)), with the token flow following the correct-after-align strategy: alignment via DAA first, then attention score correction via MDM.

An analysis module is implemented to visualize and compute the attention score distribution across modalities and layers, measuring the average difference between self-modal and cross-modal attention scores (as in Figure 2 and Figure 4).

The ablation study is executed successfully at 8B scale with 700K training samples across all four variant groups in Table 1 (module, alignment, fusion, mask), evaluating on general QA, knowledge QA, OCR&Chart QA, and vision-centric QA tasks.

The main perception experiments are executed successfully on the 16 benchmarks (MME, MMBench, SEED, GQA, ScienceQA, MMMU, MathVista, AI2D, ChartQA, OCRBench, TextVQA, DocVQA, MMVP, RealworldQA, CV-Bench-2D, CV-Bench-3D) at both 8B and 34B scales.

The cognition experiments are executed successfully on the MMRole benchmark evaluating all 8 aspects: instruction adherence, fluency, coherency, image-text relevance, response accuracy, personality consistency, knowledge consistency, and tone consistency.

The emotion experiments are executed successfully on MVSA-S, MVSA-M, TumEmo, and HFM benchmarks reporting both ACC and F1 metrics, covering both general MLLM setting and emotion-specialized tuning setting.

The attention distribution analysis experiment is executed comparing baseline multimodal attention vs. MODA, measuring and reporting the average difference between self-modal and cross-modal attention across all layers (Figure 4).

The ablation results match Table 1(a): jointly using MDM and DAA achieves best performance (69.3/48.3/67.0/54.3) on G/K/O/V tasks, outperforming DAA alone (67.8/47.6/63.3/48.1), MDM alone (69.2/45.4/60.9/42.6), and baseline (63.6/44.0/60.8/38.0).

The ablation results match Table 1(b): the CoV alignment variant achieves comparable best performance on vision-centric (54.3) while GeLU achieves slightly better on knowledge (49.1), confirming CoV is selected as the final design balancing all tasks.

The ablation results match Table 1(c): concatenation fusion (Con: 69.3/48.3/67.0/54.3) outperforms additive fusion (Add: 62.2/47.6/67.2/52.2) and both single-stream alternatives (X_p, X_a) on G/K/O/V tasks.

The ablation results match Table 1(d): the attention-based mask (Attn.) achieves best vision-centric score (54.3) among all mask variants (Inf: 48.1, Fix: 52.3, [M]: 46.0), confirming it as the optimal mask design.

The perception results match Table 2 for MODA-8B: knowledge avg 61.5, OCR avg 72.0, vision avg 66.0, outperforming Cambrian-1-8B (61.3/71.3/65.0) and LLaVA-NeXT-8B (55.6/63.9/56.6) on knowledge, OCR, and vision-centric categories.
The perception results match Table 2 for MODA-34B: knowledge avg 69.5, OCR avg 74.7, vision avg 69.9, surpassing LLaVA-NeXT-34B (62.5/67.7/64.0) and comparable to Cambrian-1-34B (67.0/71.9/68.5).

The cognition results match Table 3: MODA-8B achieves avg 0.972 in the general setting, outperforming LLaVA-NeXT-8B (0.968) and Cambrian-1-8B (0.895); with cognition-specific tuning MODA-8B achieves avg 0.995, surpassing MMRole-9B (0.994).

The emotion results match Table 4: MODA-8B (general) achieves avg 0.588 outperforming Cambrian-1-8B (0.547) and LLaVA-NeXT-8B (0.576); with emotion-specific tuning MODA-8B achieves avg 0.841, surpassing SPFVTE on MVSA-S (0.810 vs 0.806) and MULSER on TumEmo (0.778 vs 0.775).

The attention analysis matches Figure 4: baseline multimodal attention shows average difference of 56.97% for text and 62.44% for visual between self- and cross-modal attention; MODA reduces this disparity to 50.31% for text and 41.01% for visual.
